metadata.title: Air Operated Diaphragm Pumps | Welltech®
metadata.description: Non-electric, high-suction stainless steel air operated diaphragm pumps for explosive and flammable environments (ATEX).

hero.title: Air Operated Diaphragm Pumps
hero.desc: Zero stalling guarantee for fluids with solid particles and high viscosity, with ATEX approval thanks to its non-electric (pneumatic) operating principle.

safety.title: Maximum Safety (ATEX)
safety.desc: The air-operated (pneumatic) working principle, which does not contain an electric motor, makes these pumps the single and safest option in transferring flammable solvents and explosive chemicals. It can tolerate transferring liquids with solid particles or dry running conditions without breaking down. Provides uninterrupted and safe production with its non-stalling air valve system.

features.title: Design and Hardware Advantages
items: Ability to Transfer Solid Particles Without Jamming
Low Operating Cost Due to Non-Electric Operation
Easy Installation, Low Spare Parts and Maintenance Needs
features.imgAlt: Air Operated Diaphragm Pump Valve Mechanism

table.title: Performance Curve and Capacity Table
table.note: * Given fluid values are calculated based on water (1 cPs). Pump sizes may change for highly viscous and particulate fluids.
table.imgAlt: Air Operated Diaphragm Pump Technical Drawing

items.q: How is the diaphragm material selected?
items.a: Teflon (PTFE), Santoprene, NBR, or Hytrel diaphragms are selected according to the pH (acidity) level and temperature of the fluid to be transferred by the pump.

items.q: What is the advantage of the pump being air-operated?
items.a: Since it does not need an electrical grid, the risk of explosion is zero. Furthermore, when the valve is closed, the pump stops on its own and does not require costly additional protection equipment like a pressure bypass valve.

reference.desc: A 40-unit diaphragm pump line we installed for an international paint manufacturing plant.

cta.title: Which Diaphragm is Suitable?
cta.desc: PTFE, NBR, or Santoprene? Let us choose the most durable diaphragm material based on your fluid's (MSDS) report.
